title: Reference Counting, Weak References, and Cycles · C Memory Workbench
summary: A guided implementation pair receives a clean input trace and acceptance examples, then builds the first working overflow-safe reference-counted graph while narrating each size, lifetime, ownership, and cleanup decision. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of overflow-safe reference-counted graph while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Maintain a reference count equal to defined strong ownership edges and release exactly when the last edge ends.
Prevent count overflow, underflow, resurrection, and release reentrancy with explicit states.
Demonstrate why pure reference counting cannot reclaim strong cycles and locate cycle-forming edges.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Commit to the mission model · Workshop Retain Release Invariant
steps.instruction:
workshop case — A workshop native-systems team handles memory case 1130. Produce bounded C evidence to model strong counts and expose a cycle that cannot reach zero; change the size, lifetime, failure, or ownership condition; print the observed result and invariant. Instant code stays inside the PicoC practice subset. C23 syntax, current GCC and Clang, optimization, ABI, threads, sanitizers, analyzers, fuzzers, operating-system resources, foreign runtimes, load, and production behavior require named controlled transfer gates.

What must be established before implementation?

options.text: For test an acceptance boundary, run one convenient Threaded Refcount Cost case, omit size, bounds, lifetime, owner, allocation failure, cleanup, invariant output, and native transfer gates, then call the browser result production-safe. This does not satisfy Threaded Refcount Cost for overflow-safe reference-counted graph.
Reject the unsupported conclusion, name the violated Threaded Refcount Cost condition, repair the responsible layer, and rerun the boundary trace.

Keep the plausible Threaded Refcount Cost output and hide the violated overflow-safe reference-counted graph assumption instead of explaining the failure. That claim fails the test an acceptance boundary evidence boundary for Threaded Refcount Cost.

steps.title: Defend the transferred model · Workshop Threaded Refcount Cost
steps.instruction:
Write the workshop evidence note for Threaded Refcount Cost: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — A workshop native-systems team handles memory case 1937. Produce bounded C evidence to model strong counts and expose a cycle that cannot reach zero; change the size, lifetime, failure, or ownership condition; print the observed result and invariant. Instant code stays inside the PicoC practice subset. C23 syntax, current GCC and Clang, optimization, ABI, threads, sanitizers, analyzers, fuzzers, operating-system resources, foreign runtimes, load, and production behavior require named controlled transfer gates. Competency probe: Evaluate atomicity, contention, batching, deferred release, and memory-order requirements for shared counts.

Evidence target: Defend the transferred model · Workshop Threaded Refcount Cost.
steps.why: Retrieving Threaded Refcount Cost through the concrete overflow-safe reference-counted graph case exposes causal gaps that recognition alone can hide. For Defend the transferred model · Workshop Threaded Refcount Cost, keep Threaded Refcount Cost connected to executable C memory invariant and explicit native transfer evidence.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Evaluate atomicity, contention, batching, deferred release, and memory-order requirements for shared counts.
